common_name: Great Solomon's Seal (Giant Native Woodland Solomon's Seal)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in partial to full shade; plant rhizomes horizontally just below the soil surface, 2–3 inches deep; Great Solomon's Seal spreads slowly by rhizomes to form expanding colonies.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Great Solomon's Seal is the largest North American native Solomon's seal — a magnificent woodland plant producing arching stems 4–7 feet tall (significantly larger than the common Solomon's seal, P. biflorum, which reaches only 2–3 feet) hung with pairs of pendulous, creamy-white, bell-shaped flowers in late spring, followed by ornamental blue-black berries relished by birds in fall. The large, boldly-oval, alternate leaves arranged along the gracefully arching stems create an architectural, tropical effect that is one of the most dramatic structure elements available for the native shade garden. The arching form and large leaves provide outstanding textural contrast with fine-textured groundcovers, ferns, and hostas in the shade border. Great Solomon's Seal spreads slowly by underground rhizomes to form expanding colonies that naturalize effectively under the canopy of deciduous trees, creating an increasingly impressive woodland garden planting over years. The native habitat of creek banks and moist woodland edges informs its garden requirements: consistent moisture and humus-rich, acidic soil are essential for the best performance, though established plants show reasonable drought tolerance once roots are deeply established. In zone 9, it thrives under tall pines and deciduous hardwoods where summer shade and organic leaf-litter soil accumulate.

common_name: Berry Smoothie Heuchera (Pink-Purple Raspberry Coral Bell)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; in zone 9, heucheras benefit from afternoon shade protection — the bright foliage colors fade and the plants decline in intense afternoon sun combined with Gulf South summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Berry Smoothie is one of the most vividly colored heuchera introductions — producing large, rounded, ruffled leaves in a rich, warm raspberry-pink to deep rose-pink color that is more saturated and vivid than the softer peachy tones of 'Georgia Peach' and the cooler tones of 'Caramel'. The warm raspberry-pink foliage creates a strong color focal point in the partial-shade garden with a boldness that few other heucheras match. The metallic, slightly reflective surface of the leaves catches available light in shade conditions, making Berry Smoothie one of the brightest and most visible heucheras in low-light situations. The color typically deepens to burgundy-raspberry in cooler fall and winter temperatures and lightens slightly in the summer heat. In early summer, small, airy white flower sprays rise above the foliage on slender scapes — delicate accents to the dominant foliage display. Heuchera hybrids like Berry Smoothie are essential partial-shade perennials for zone 9 gardens — they provide multi-season foliage color in areas where most other plants offer only green. Divide heucheras every 3–4 years in early spring when the crown becomes too elevated above the soil surface and performance declines. In zone 9, plant in the shadiest locations available, with consistent moisture and excellent drainage.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Carnival's cream-variegated foliage brightens in partial shade — full afternoon sun in zone 9 may bleach the cream margins to white and scorch the leaf tips.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Carnival is a variegated liriope cultivar with cream to creamy-white longitudinal striping on dark-green, strap-like leaves — forming compact, mounding clumps of distinctive two-tone foliage that brightens shade garden areas with the cream-on-green contrast effect common to effective shade variegated plants. Carnival's pink to rose-pink flower spikes (a warmer, pinker color than the purple-violet of most liriope cultivars) rise above the variegated foliage in summer and create an attractive three-color combination of cream-striped green leaves, pink flower spikes, and eventually blue-black berries in fall. The pink flowers rather than typical purple flowers are Carnival's most distinctive attribute among variegated liriope selections — the flower color combines with the cream foliage variegation to create a lighter, airier, more pastel effect than dark-purple-flowered types. Like all liriopes, Carnival is adaptable and low-maintenance in zone 9 — tolerating heat, drought, shade, and root competition from trees while remaining evergreen through mild Gulf South winters. The variegated foliage is most effective when mass-planted in sizeable groupings where the light-reflective cream-and-green texture creates a visual effect visible from a distance. Shear to 3 inches in late winter to remove old foliage before new spring growth emerges.

common_name: Emerald Blue Creeping Phlox (Sky-Blue Moss Phlox)

planting.note: Plant container plants in late winter or early spring in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; Emerald Blue performs best in sharply drained, lean soil — rich, moist soil promotes lush foliage growth at the expense of flowering and increases susceptibility to crown rot.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Emerald Blue is the standard sky-blue cultivar of Phlox subulata (moss phlox or creeping phlox) — a low, mat-forming native perennial that in early spring (March–April in zone 7–8) produces such a profusion of lavender-blue flowers that the entire plant is obscured under a carpet of blooms. The color of Emerald Blue is a clear, mid-blue lavender — lighter and more purely blue than purple cultivars like 'Red Wings' and 'Scarlet Flame', creating a soft, cool, sky-blue effect that photographs beautifully and reads as a gentle blue sweep across rockeries, slopes, and garden borders. The very dense, needle-like, evergreen foliage forms a tight, weed-suppressing mat year-round; after the brief but spectacular spring bloom, the plant remains attractive as a low, fine-textured, deep-green groundcover through summer, fall, and winter. Creeping phlox is outstanding for slopes, embankments, and rock gardens where its spreading mat anchors soil and outcompetes weeds. Shear lightly after bloom (to 2–3 inches) to encourage dense new growth and a more compact habit. In zone 9, creeping phlox blooms in late February through March — very early in the Gulf South spring — and then serves as an unobtrusive fine-textured groundcover through the warm season. Sharp drainage is the single most critical requirement.

common_name: Candy Stripe Creeping Phlox (Bicolor White-Pink Moss Phlox)

planting.note: Plant container plants in late winter or early spring in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Candy Stripe requires the same sharp drainage as all creeping phlox — avoid heavy clay or consistently moist soil, which promotes crown rot and decline.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Candy Stripe is the most distinctive bicolor creeping phlox cultivar — producing flowers with a clean white base and a prominent, vivid pink-rose stripe down the center of each petal, creating a candy-stripe pattern that gives each flower an unusual, ornate appearance unlike the solid-colored flowers of most Phlox subulata cultivars. The bicolor striped effect is most vivid when flowers are fully open — the strong contrast between white and pink creates a more complex, visually interesting floral texture across the blooming mat than solid pink or solid white cultivars. In spring (March–April in zone 7–8, February–March in zone 9), the entire plant disappears under a carpet of bicolor flowers — the candy-stripe pattern visible as a mass effect creates a distinctive pink-flushed-white wash rather than either solid pink or solid white. Like all Phlox subulata cultivars, Candy Stripe forms a dense, evergreen, needle-like mat (4–6 inches tall) that is effective as a year-round groundcover on slopes, in rock gardens, and spilling over walls. The sharp drainage requirement is non-negotiable — creeping phlox in heavy clay or consistently moist soil develops crown rot and declines within 1–2 seasons. In zone 9, Candy Stripe performs reliably when sited in well-drained, raised, or slope positions where soil dries between rain events.
